config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because individual rights are what protect people from abuse by the majority or by the state. In my view, society is healthier when basic freedoms are treated as a hard limit, not something that can be sacrificed whenever it is convenient. Of course, individual rights are not absolute in every situation, but the burden should be on society to justify any restriction, not the other way around.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not automatically take priority over the individual, because once that idea becomes too broad, it can be used to excuse real harm and suppress dissent. From my perspective as someone from China, I do understand why people emphasize social stability, collective responsibility, and the common good, since those values can matter a lot in a large society. But I do not think that gives society a blank check to override individual rights whenever it claims to have a better purpose. The fairest approach is to balance both, with strong limits on what society can demand from a person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how easy it is for “the common good” to become a vague excuse for silencing criticism, protecting power, or pressuring people to conform. Social harmony is important, but real harmony is not built by sacrificing basic rights every time there is a conflict. I would put it this way: society can restrict individual freedom only when the restriction is necessary, narrowly justified, and still respects human dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective in China, I understand the appeal of prioritizing social order, because people do value stability, coordination, and avoiding chaos, especially in a large and complex country. But I have also seen how easily “society” can become a convenient excuse for weakening dissent, privacy, and fairness, so I do not trust that claim when it is used too broadly. A society is strongest when it protects basic individual rights first and only limits them in narrow, clearly justified cases.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective as someone from Indonesia, the phrase “for the common good” is often used too loosely, and I have seen how easily it can be turned into pressure to stay quiet, conform, or accept unfair treatment. Social harmony is important, yes, but harmony built on silencing people is shallow and fragile. I would only accept limits on individual rights when they are necessary, narrowly defined, and genuinely accountable, not just because society says it wants order.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule, because that principle is too easy to abuse. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why people value stability, coordination, and collective responsibility, but I have also seen how “the common good” can be used to justify suppressing dissent, privacy, and fairness. Social order matters, yet real order is stronger when basic rights are protected first and limits are allowed only in narrow, clearly justified cases. If society can override individuals whenever it claims a higher purpose, then individual rights stop being real rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
